FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study data used; baseline (Y0; ages 9-10) to year two follow-up (Y2; ages 11-12)

FT_IC2.evidence: Addictive phone use, addictive social media use, addictive videogaming, and composite measure of ASU reported as separate variables. Applies B1 (YSTS with separate β for SM, phone, videogaming) and B6 (mobile app gaming/videogaming with separate coefficients)

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects regression with mixed-effects regressions conducted for both cortical connectomic maturation (AFC) and reward pathway (NAc anticipatory activation) as predictors of addictive screen use measures. Permutation p-values reported for AFC analyses; Holm-Bonferroni correction applied

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients reported for all analyses: AFC vs cross-domain ASU β_M1 = -.056, CI [-.083, -.027], p < .001; addictive videogaming β_M1 = -.060, CI [-.087, -.031], p < .001; addictive phone use β_M1 = -.039, CI [-.069, -.008], p = .035; NAc activation β_uae = -.023, CI [-.045, -.006], p = .029. Indirect effects reported via 1000 bootstrapped mixed-effects regressions

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal ABCD analysis predicting Y2 addictive screen use from Y0 brain measures (cortical connectomic maturation, reward activation)

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate; original published article (accepted 11 March 2026)

FT_EC7_reason: Addictive screen use is the DEPENDENT variable, but FT-EC7 does NOT apply because: (a) predictor variables include neural features (cortical connectomic maturation, NAc activation) which are explicitly NOT in the demographic-only set per FT-EC7(b); violates B17 exclusion (neural features as predictors disqualify). Analysis is longitudinal but includes non-demographic predictors, so FT-EC7 condition (b) fails
notes: All three ASU domains (videogaming, social media, phone) analyzed with separate coefficients. Study uses Anchored Functional Connectomic maturation score (AFC) and MID task-based NAc activation—neurobiological predictors, not demographic. Cortical maturation association survives control for total screen time, attention problems, and general psychopathology, showing independent contribution

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study; n=6,467 youth (39.2% female) ages 12 at baseline assessed at ages 12-13

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic gaming measured at years 2 and 3 using Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (Bagot et al., 2022). Applies B1 (YSTS reporting separate coefficient for problematic gaming as IV with other environmental predictors)

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed models with hierarchical regression to examine associations between problematic gaming (year 2) and psychotic-like experiences (year 3). Moderation analysis testing interaction of problematic gaming with peer/school/family environment variables

FT_IC4.evidence: Estimates reported (95% CI) for main associations and moderation models: association between problematic gaming at Y2 and PLEs at Y3 (estimate [95% CI] = 0.13 [0.09, 0.17], p < 0.001). Moderation coefficients for school and family environment interactions provided in tables (Fig. 2)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective longitudinal ABCD analysis examining problematic gaming at age 12 predicting psychotic-like experiences at age 13

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original research (accepted 7 December 2025); no evidence of preprint/duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Problematic gaming is the INDEPENDENT VARIABLE predicting psychotic-like experiences (the outcome/DV). FT-EC7 applies only when gaming/SM is the DV; this paper analyzes gaming → PLE, not gaming as outcome. Additionally, the analysis includes non-demographic predictors (peer environment, school environment, family environment), violating FT-EC7(b) demographic-only criterion. Condition (a) fails: gaming is not the DV
notes: Paper uses affordance theory framework to examine whether environmental factors moderate the problematic gaming-PLE association. Peer environment (number of close friends) was not associated with PLE; school and family environments were protective against problematic gaming but did NOT attenuate the gaming-PLE link. All analyses adjusted for age, sex, parental education, income-to-needs, ethnicity, family history of psychosis, parental separation

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study; 2,294 participants (Mage=10.03, 50.6% female) at baseline with resting-state fMRI at baseline and PUSM scores at four-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic use of social media (PUSM) measured at four-year follow-up using 6-item Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ). Applies B1 (YSTS/ML reporting coefficient for SM-specific measure as outcome, independent of other screen use domains)

FT_IC3.evidence: Connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM) with machine learning; Pearson's r correlation between observed and predicted SMAQ scores used to assess model performance with permutation testing (1000 permutations for statistical significance)

FT_IC4.evidence: CPM successfully predicted PUSM severity: combined positive and negative networks r=0.055, p=0.016 (when controlling for covariates); permutation testing p=0.02. Edge-level analyses identified 2,507 edges (1,212 positive, 1,295 negative) comprising predictive networks. Functional connectivity coefficients reported for resting-state correlation matrices

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal ABCD analysis using CPM on baseline rs-fMRI data (age 9-10) to predict PUSM severity at four-year follow-up (age 13-14)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original research in NeuroImage (accepted 22 February 2026); no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: PUSM is the DEPENDENT VARIABLE being predicted. However, FT-EC7 does NOT apply because: (a) the predictors are neural features (whole-brain resting-state functional connectivity patterns), NOT demographics. FT-EC7(b) requires predictors be exclusively in the demographic-only closed set; neural connectivity violates this per B17 (neural features as predictors disqualify). Condition (b) fails explicitly
notes: Machine learning approach (CPM) using penalized methodology; B8 applies (traditional regression coefficients appear—r/p values and connectivity edge weights). Two key neural systems identified: (i) cognitive control/self-regulation system (DMN, medial frontal, frontoparietal networks) with decreased connectivity predictive of PUSM; (ii) perceptual-motor integration system (visual area 1, sensorimotor networks) with increased connectivity predictive of PUSM. Covariates controlled: age, sex, race/ethnicity, parental marital status, parental education, family income, mean frame-wise displacement
